title: Areas of Trapezoids, Rhombi, and Kites

summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the area of a trapezoid with bases 8 cm and 14 cm and a height of 5 cm?

Area of a Trapezoid A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h where b₁ and b₂ are the parallel bases and h is the height. The other options — '110 cm²', '70 cm²', and '44 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

A rhombus has diagonals of length 10 cm and 16 cm. What is its area?

Area of a Rhombus or Kite A = ½d₁d₂ where d₁ and d₂ are the lengths of the diagonals. Key Ideas The diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular bisectors of each other. The other options — '160 cm²', '40 cm²', and '120 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is the area of a kite with diagonals of 12 m and 7 m?

Area of a Rhombus or Kite A = ½d₁d₂ where d₁ and d₂ are the lengths of the diagonals. The diagonals of a kite are perpendicular, but only one is bisected. The other options — '84 m²', '38 m²', and '19 m²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

A trapezoid has a median of 10 cm and a height of 8 cm. What is its area?

Area of a Trapezoid A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h where b₁ and b₂ are the parallel bases and h is the height. The other options — '40 cm²', '160 cm²', and '100 cm²' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.
